data.reasoning: Both models skipped the only reviewed file (PKGBUILD) due to a false 'binary file' detection. No content was actually analyzed by either model — 0 files reviewed, 0 tokens consumed. The PKGBUILD is almost certainly a normal text file that was misclassified as binary, likely due to an encoding heuristic failure (e.g., non-ASCII characters, BOM, or null-byte detection edge case). Without any actual review of the PKGBUILD or accompanying scripts/install hooks, no safety determination can be made.
data.disagreement_analysis: There is no disagreement — both models produced identical SKIPPED verdicts due to the same tooling-level binary-file misdetection, not differing reasoning.
coverage_issues: PKGBUILD was never reviewed (misclassified as binary)
No .install hooks, source files, or auxiliary scripts were enumerated or reviewed — the tool stopped at the initial PKGBUILD gate
Entire package effectively unaudited (0 files reviewed across both models)
false_positives: 'Skipping binary file: PKGBUILD' — PKGBUILDs are shell scripts (text); this is a tool-level false positive in the file-type classifier, not a security finding
false_negatives: Unknown — because nothing was reviewed, any real risk in PKGBUILD (network fetches, sources= URLs, build()/package() commands, .install scripts) would be entirely missed
re_audit_focus: PKGBUILD (force text decoding / fix binary-detection heuristic before audit)
sources= array URLs and integrity (this is a 'nightly' package — likely pulls remote artifacts at build time)
build(), prepare(), package(), check() functions for download/exec patterns
learnings: The binary-file detection heuristic is misclassifying PKGBUILD (a known text/shell file). PKGBUILD and .install files should be whitelisted as text and never skipped as binary.
When the initial PKGBUILD gate fails due to a SKIP (not a security concern), the tool should retry with forced UTF-8/latin-1 decoding rather than aborting the entire audit.
A skip on the critical gating file should not silently end the audit with 0 files reviewed — this should escalate to mandatory re-audit, which the trigger correctly caught.
'Nightly' self-hosted packages commonly fetch remote binaries at build time; the tool should flag these for extra scrutiny once content is actually readable.
Investigate whether the PKGBUILD contains a BOM, null bytes, or unusual encoding that trips the classifier — worth logging the specific bytes that triggered 'binary' detection for diagnosis.

data.reasoning: Both models skipped the critical PKGBUILD file by misclassifying it as binary, leaving no basis for safety assessment. No other files were reviewed.
coverage_issues: No review of .install hooks or source files with execution risks
false_negatives: Entire package risk assessment missed due to PKGBUILD skip
learnings: Prioritize PKGBUILD as mandatory first file; never skip it
Improve binary detection: PKGBUILD is always text
Abort audit if PKGBUILD can't be read instead of skipping
Add fallback mechanism for misclassified critical files
